comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the potential and challenges of using drones for utility inspections, with several commenters sharing relevant industry insights and use cases. Key themes include regulatory hurdles, data analytics, and the value of real-time, high-quality data for infrastructure maintenance.

comments.author: Uncle_Clark
comments.text: I work for a utility company and this is brilliant. One other issue that comes into play for inspections is right of way. My understanding of this is a bit fuzzy but is very interesting. Back in the day, if a power company had a transmission line on the property, they could just go out and service it. With modern infrastructure frequently being on top of one another, power lines, internet cables, pipes, this becomes a more complicated issue and so property management and the ability to access sites becomes a question. Drones can frequently go where people can't so in thick vegetation I can see this really working for surveying. Very cool.
comments.analysis: This comment provides practical insight from industry experience, highlighting a real operational challenge and how drones can address it, making it highly credible and relevant.

comments.author: petargyurov
comments.text:
At my job [0] we work in this exact space, but on the data analytics side of things. We build computer vision pipelines for corrosion, components, defects, etc. We process imagery from drones, helis and satellite.

This is a tough problem to solve and for a lot of operators ultimately comes down to cost.

We're based in the UK but reach out if you wanna chat!

[0] https://keen-ai.com/
comments.analysis: This comment adds depth by discussing the data analytics aspect of drone inspections, confirming the complexity and cost considerations involved, which aligns well with industry realities.

comments.author: contingencies
comments.text: Love the name. Perhaps an angle you haven't considered is rapid quantitative data gathering for site inspection for rapid setup of non-traditional infrastructure like wind and solar. Should be zeitgeist with gas prices shooting through the roof. By actually being 250-400ft above the proposed site and taking real measurements you get actionable insights. Multi-season survey ideal but raw data from a specific site has gravitas and seasonal inference is straightforward within a confidence interval.
comments.analysis: Offers a valuable perspective on expanding drone utility to renewable energy infrastructure, which is a plausible and relevant application given current energy trends.

comments.author: zanderz
comments.text: I got a survey of my residential property, 1/2 acre on a rugged hillside, from a guy who used a drone equipped with lidar. It was $4,000, less than traditional methods, but it would be cool if a drone could just be dispatched from a nearby power pole instead.
comments.analysis: Provides a concrete example of drone surveying with lidar, supporting the claim that drones can be cost-effective alternatives to traditional methods.

comments.author: nhecker
comments.text: Sounds like a neat space to be in. Wishing you calm skies next month. I guess you'll have all the goodies (RFI detectors, thermal imaging sensors, etc.) to collect data? I wonder to what extent detection from a distance (discovery?) and investigation of faults can be automated. Hopefully using drones for good will be sufficient for a viable business model. We've enough surveillance as it is.
comments.analysis: Raises relevant questions about the technology and automation potential, showing curiosity and constructive engagement with the topic.

comments.author: codingrightnow
comments.text: How does this get around the problem you mentioned with FAA line of sight regulations?
comments.analysis: Poses a critical regulatory question that is central to drone operations, highlighting a significant practical challenge.

comments.author: wangmander
comments.text: Is the inspection data you're accumulating becoming a moat in itself?
comments.analysis: Insightfully questions the competitive advantage of data accumulation, which is an important strategic consideration.

comments.author: pokot0
comments.text:
You guys probably already know but I saw a live demo of something very similar from these guys:

https://bright.ai/autonomous-inspection/

definitely where we want to be as a society! Keep up!
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant external example, contributing to the discussion by pointing to similar existing technology.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
I worked at a place that provided services to railroad owners. This was back in the 90s, but they had camera rigs on engines that would record multiple angles along the train routes. They would store that footage in massive tape libraries to be used as reference to see the terrain in an area that service was needed to they'd know what kind of equipment would be necessary as well as a visual of the tracks themselves. They could not update the footage as fast as they liked. IIRC, footage was over a year old before getting updated.

Just another suggestion of a sector you could gain traction as it wasn't listed.
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and a related use case, suggesting potential expansion areas for drone inspection technology.

comments.author: igor47
comments.text: No feedback, this just sounds really cool.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm but adds no substantive information or critique.
